planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; marsh violet thrives in the consistently moist to wet conditions of stream banks, pond margins, and rain garden areas where most violets would rot.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — marsh violet is the native violet of wet, shaded environments, growing naturally along stream banks, pond margins, seepage areas, and wet woodland clearings where it produces blue-violet flowers with distinctive darker veining and a white base on the lower petals, held on stems above the characteristic heart-shaped leaves from spring through early summer. It is distinguished from the common blue violet (V. sororia) by its requirement for consistently moist to wet soil and its preference for shaded sites. An outstanding native for rain garden margins, wet woodland gardens, pond edges, and boggy areas in partial to full shade where few flowering plants thrive. Like all violets, a critical larval host plant for native fritillary butterflies. Forms attractive colonies in appropriate moist, shaded conditions by self-seeding and spreading by runners.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; cream violet is one of the most adaptable native violets for the shaded garden, tolerating a range of moisture conditions and significant shade.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — cream violet is a native woodland violet of eastern deciduous forests, distinguished by its cream to white flowers with distinctive purple veining (striping) on the lower three petals that gives it both the species name striata and the common name striped cream violet. Unlike the stemless violets (V. sororia, V. cucullata), cream violet is leafy-stemmed, growing from branched stems rather than a basal rosette, which gives it a somewhat different growth form in the woodland garden. It blooms in spring and often reblooms again in fall. Grows naturally in moist, rich woodland, stream banks, and moist forest clearings throughout the eastern US. Forms attractive groundcover colonies in shaded settings. Like all native violets, it supports native fritillary butterfly caterpillars as a larval host plant. An excellent white-flowered native violet alternative to the more common blue species.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; long-spurred violet is an Appalachian mountain species that performs best in cool, moist, shaded conditions with good drainage and slightly acidic soil.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade — long-spurred violet is one of the most distinctive and elegant of all native violets, immediately recognizable by its unusually long nectar spur — 0.5 to 0.75 inches long, compared to the very short spurs of most violets — and its pale lavender flowers with darker veining and a darker eye. The long nectar spur specifically coevolved with certain long-tongued bees and butterflies that can access the nectar. It grows naturally in cool, moist, rich Appalachian woodland from Pennsylvania south to the Carolinas, preferring the well-drained, acidic, forest soils of mountain slopes and ravine banks. Less adaptable to hot, humid lowland conditions than most native violets. A beautiful specialty violet for cool-climate woodland gardens and native plant collections in zones 4–8, and a host plant for native fritillary butterflies.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile, humus-rich soil in full sun to partial shade; hollow Joe-Pye weed is the tallest of the common Joe-Pye weeds — regularly reaching 6–12 feet in moist, fertile soil — and requires adequate moisture to achieve its impressive stature.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — hollow Joe-Pye weed is the largest native Joe-Pye weed, regularly reaching 5–12 feet in moist, fertile conditions, making it one of the most imposing native perennials in the eastern flora. It is identifiable by its hollow, purple-spotted stems (the 'fistulosum' meaning tubular, and the purple nodes). Large, domed clusters of mauve-pink flowers in midsummer to late summer are massively attractive to migrating monarchs, eastern tiger swallowtails, fritillaries, and dozens of native bee species — few plants concentrate as many pollinators in a single location as a blooming Joe-Pye weed. It grows naturally along stream banks, moist meadows, and wet open woodland edges throughout the eastern US. Named varieties including 'Gateway' (more compact, 5–6 feet) are widely available. Outstanding back-of-border or screening native for moist, sunny to partly shaded gardens.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; green dragon is closely related to jack-in-the-pulpit but differs in having a single large compound leaf and a dramatically elongated spadix protruding 3–6 inches from the spathe.

directions: Plant corms in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — green dragon is the less-commonly-grown native relative of jack-in-the-pulpit, equally bizarre and fascinating in flower but with a different structure: a single large compound leaf (7–15 leaflets radiating from the tip of the stem) and a distinctive flower featuring a narrow, elongated spadix that projects 3–6 inches beyond the green spathe like the tongue of a dragon — giving both the common name and the specific epithet dracontium. The bright red berries clustered on the spadix in late summer are strikingly ornamental. It grows naturally in moist, rich, shaded bottomland forests and stream banks throughout the eastern US. The corms and all parts of the plant contain calcium oxalate crystals and are toxic if consumed raw; like jack-in-the-pulpit, historically used by Native American nations after careful preparation to neutralize the crystals. Outstanding bold-textured native for shaded, moist woodland gardens.
